definition_origin: A mascot is a personified brand character used as a recurring visual presence across a product — Duolingo's Duo, Mailchimp's Freddie, GitHub's Octocat, Reddit's Snoo, Discord's Wumpus, Trello's Taco, Slack's Slackbot. Mascots emerged in advertising (Mr. Peanut 1916, Tony the Tiger 1952) and migrated into software branding starting with BSD's Beastie and the GNU gnu (1980s) — early developer mascots were inside jokes that grew into identity. Modern app mascots fuse anthropomorphism research (Epley/Waytz, 2007 — assigning human traits to non-humans drives parasocial bonding) with character-bible production discipline borrowed from animation studios (model sheets, expression sheets, color/shape rules).
core_purpose: **Visceral**: mascots make a brand instantly recognizable and emotionally warm — the difference between 'an app' and 'a friend who lives in an app.' **Behavioral**: a mascot is the voice in tooltips, error states, empty states, and onboarding — it carries tone-of-voice work that pure UI copy cannot. Duolingo's Duo turning passive-aggressive in push notifications IS the brand. **Reflective**: returning users build a parasocial relationship — they see Duo's manic eyes, the dopamine of 'oh, my owl friend' fires before any feature is used. For an archetype-driven product, the mascot can carry archetype tension that the rest of the UI cannot — Magician (mysterious) wrapped in Caregiver (warm) is hard to do in shape language alone, but trivial when one cute character has subtly arcane eyes. The mascot is also the cheapest illustration system: instead of commissioning 200 unique illustrations, you draw the mascot in 200 poses and the brand stays cohesive automatically.
when_needed: **Strongly recommended** for consumer mobile apps with high engagement frequency (daily/weekly), education/learning, kids/family, fitness, mental health, language, finance-for-Gen-Z. **Optional but powerful** for B2B/dev-tool apps where you want emotional warmth in a category that's all enterprise grey (GitHub Octocat, MongoDB Leaf, Postman Astronaut). **Risky** for serious B2B (legal, healthcare, finance enterprise) where a mascot signals 'unserious.' **Mostly skip** for AI-wrapper apps unless your archetype is explicitly Jester/Creator — most AI tools (Cursor, Linear, Vercel) deliberately avoid mascots to read as 'serious tools.' For our user's Magician/Creator + Jester/Outlaw + Caregiver lean across consumer mobile + B2B + AI-wrapper, the answer differs per app — consumer mobile likely yes, B2B optional, AI-wrapper probably no.
format_options: **Source vector** (.fig, .ai, .svg) — the character bible lives here, with poses on artboards, expression sheets, and color tokens. Vector lets you scale to any size and re-color algorithmically (light/dark mode). **Static raster** (.png, .webp) for app surfaces — typical asset is 240x240 or 480x480 PNG with alpha. **Animated**: Lottie (.json) for short ambient loops (~30-100KB) — Duolingo uses Lottie heavily; Rive (.riv, ~5-20KB) for state-machine interactivity (the mascot reacting to user state — looking around, blinking when idle, celebrating on streak). **3D**: glTF/USDZ if you go full 3D (Discord Wumpus has 3D variants for their splash); only worth it if you have a 3D pipeline. **AI character source**: a trained LoRA (.safetensors, 100-300MB) or 5-10 reference images for IP-Adapter is your reproducible bottle for AI-assisted mascot generation. Pros/cons: vector = endlessly editable but needs designer to make new poses; Lottie = beautiful but requires After Effects knowledge OR a designer to author; Rive = smaller and interactive but newer ecosystem; 3D = expensive overkill for solo dev. **Recommendation for our user**: vector master in Figma + Lottie via LottieFiles community + Rive for one or two key state-machine moments.
file_size_performance: PNG mascot: 50-150KB at 480x480 (compress with cwebp -q 85 to drop to 30-80KB WebP). Lottie: target under 100KB, ideal under 50KB; strip hidden layers and use lottie-optimizer. Rive: typically 5-30KB even with state machines. SVG: 10-50KB raw, drop to 5-20KB after SVGO. Avoid GIF (5-10x larger than Lottie for same content). For 3D glTF: 200KB-2MB; only justified for hero placement.
free_tools: 1. **Figma Free** — primary mascot bible authoring (single designer, 3 file limit but 1 mascot file is enough). 2. **Inkscape (FREE, open source)** — full vector alternative if Figma's 3-file cap blocks you. 3. **LottieFiles Free** — 100 active assets, free editor, marketplace of pre-built mascot animations you can fork. 4. **Rive Community (FREE)** — unlimited public files, full feature access; only constraint is files are public. For solo dev with no IP concerns, this is the highest-leverage tool. 5. **ComfyUI (FREE, runs locally on M-series Mac with 16GB+ RAM)** — open-source node-based AI workflow; the de facto standard for character-consistent generation via IP-Adapter + ControlNet + LoRA. Steep learning curve (~2-3 days). 6. **AUTOMATIC1111/Forge (FREE, local)** — simpler Stable Diffusion UI with IP-Adapter support; lower ceiling than ComfyUI but easier to start. 7. **Recraft Free (50 credits/day)** — one of the few AI tools that emits editable SVG, useful for getting mascot shapes you then refine in Figma. 8. **Google Gemini Imagen 4 (included in Gemini Pro)** — strong consistency when given a reference image via Whisk-style flow. 9. **Microsoft Designer Free** — DALL-E 3, decent for early concepts but weak character consistency. 10. **OpenPeeps, Open Doodles (FREE Pablo Stanley sets)** — pre-built modular character pieces; warning, these have a slop signature now (see traps).
paid_tools: **Starter ($10-20/mo)**: Recraft Basic ($12/mo) for SVG-native AI output. Rive Pro ($14/mo) only if you need private files. **Pro ($20-30/mo)**: Adobe Illustrator ($22.99/mo) if you're already in CC; otherwise Figma + Inkscape covers vector work. Procreate (one-time $12.99 iPad) is the cheapest sketching tool that pros use professionally for mascot ideation. **Enterprise ($50+)**: hire a freelance illustrator on Dribbble/Behance ($300-1500 one-time for full character bible) — for a consumer brand with mascot as core differentiator, this is the single highest-ROI spend; cheaper than 6 months of subscription tooling and yields a unique IP. Topaz Photo AI ($79 one-time) for upscaling AI mascot output to print resolution. **Verdict for our broke solo dev**: Figma Free + Rive Community + Gemini Pro (already paid) + ComfyUI local. If mascot becomes a brand pillar after PMF, spend $500-1500 once on a freelance illustrator to lock the canonical bible.
gemini_pro_workflow:
**Step 1 — Brief writing with Gemini 2.5 Pro chat**: 'Help me write a character bible for a mascot. Brand archetype is {Magician/Creator + Caregiver}. App is {description}. The character must be: anthropomorphic but not human, gender-neutral, expressive enough for 6 emotions (happy, encouraging, confused, celebrating, sad, mischievous). Avoid: chibi anime tropes, generic blob with eyes, AI-default purple-pastel. Generate 8 species/concept candidates, each with one-line backstory.'

**Step 2 — Imagen 4 reference set**: Once you pick the species, generate 20-30 images with Imagen 4 in Gemini app. Prompt template: 'Character mascot for app, {species}, friendly anthropomorphic, large expressive eyes, {color palette hex}, flat vector illustration style, simple geometric shape language ({rounded squares / soft circles / sharp triangles}), front-facing portrait, no background, no text, no AI-default plastic 3D render, no melted features, hand-drawn confidence in line weight, 1024x1024.'

**Step 3 — Whisk (until 2026-04-30) / Flow (after)**: Pick the strongest 2-3 outputs, use them as Subject input. Use a real reference style (e.g., a Greg Hartman Duo screenshot you admire) as Style. Generate 10-15 variations to find your canonical front-pose.

**Step 4 — Build a Gem ('Mascot Generator')**: Custom Gemini Gem with system prompt: 'You are a character illustrator. Given a pose description, output an image of {character_name}, a {species} with {color palette}, {key visual features}, in the requested pose. Always: front- or 3/4-view, visible eye highlight, simple geometric body, flat illustration style. Never: photorealism, 3D render, gradient mesh, melted features, anime chibi.' Pin reference images. Now you have a reusable workflow.

**Step 5 — Veo for animation**: Once you have static poses, use Veo 3 to generate short 4-8 second loops — 'character {character_name} idle, gentle breathing, occasional blink, on white background, looped.' Use as Lottie/Rive reference, not as production asset (Veo output is video — convert via After Effects > Bodymovin or hand-trace in Rive).

**Step 6 — NotebookLM**: Drop in your 30 best generated images + your written character bible. Ask 'identify inconsistencies in body proportions, eye placement, color usage across these images' — Gemini will catch drift you missed.
workflow_steps: 1. **Backstory sprint (60 min)**: Write the character bible doc — name, species, age, fears, motivations, what they say in 6 canonical emotions, what they NEVER do. The Duolingo bible says Duo 'cares too much about your streak.' This is non-negotiable BEFORE pixels. 2. **Species & shape language (90 min)**: Round = friendly/Caregiver; sharp = energetic/Hero; tall = wise/Sage; stout = trustworthy. Decide one dominant shape primitive. 3. **AI exploration (90 min)**: Imagen 4 + Whisk, 50-100 candidates. Cull to 5. 4. **Vectorize the canonical pose (3-4 hours)**: Hand-redraw your favorite in Figma. This is the canonical reference image for everything that follows. 5. **Expression sheet (2-3 hours)**: 6 emotions × the same character — happy, encouraging, confused, celebrating, sad, mischievous. Same body, different face. 6. **Pose library (3-4 hours)**: 10-15 poses for app surfaces — waving (onboarding), pointing (CTA), reading (loading), sleeping (offline), high-fiving (achievement), shrugging (error), confetti (celebration). 7. **Color tokens**: define exact hex for fur/skin, eyes, accent, light/dark mode variants. 8. **AI consistency setup (4-6 hours, one-time)**: Either train a LoRA on your 30 best vector renders (using ComfyUI + Kohya, or hosted training like Replicate $5) OR build a 5-image IP-Adapter reference folder. Now AI generation produces YOUR character. 9. **Animation (2-4 hours per loop)**: Lottie idle + 2-3 reactive states. 10. **Drop into app**: Lottie components in React Native (lottie-react-native), one wrapper component `<Mascot mood='happy'/>` that swaps the asset. **Total**: ~20-30 hours for a complete first bible.
ai_prompt_templates:
**Template 1 — Canonical front pose**: '{character_name}, a friendly {species}, anthropomorphic with large expressive {color} eyes and small white highlight, body shape is {dominant primitive}, color palette {hex_a}, {hex_b}, {hex_c}, flat vector illustration with subtle hand-drawn line weight variation, front-facing portrait from chest up, neutral pleasant expression, transparent or pure white background, no shadow, no gradient mesh, no 3D render, no chibi anime style, no melted features, 1024x1024.'

**Template 2 — Emotion variant**: 'Same character {character_name} in same flat vector style, now {emotion: celebrating with arms raised / shrugging confused / encouraging with thumbs up / sleeping with z's / sad with single tear / mischievous side-eye}, identical body proportions and color palette as reference, expression conveyed primarily through eyes and mouth, transparent background.'

**Template 3 — Action pose for app surface**: '{character_name} {action: holding a magnifying glass searching / pointing right at a button / reading a book / floating with stars around / lifting heavy weights / juggling}, same flat vector illustration style as reference, character occupies center 70% of frame, {pose-appropriate accessory color}, transparent background.'

**Template 4 — IP-Adapter / Whisk reference call**: '[Reference image as Subject] — generate a new pose: {pose description}. Maintain exact: body proportions, eye style, color palette, line weight, illustration style.'

**Template 5 — LoRA-conditioned ComfyUI prompt**: '{trigger_word} character, {pose}, flat vector illustration, transparent background, masterpiece quality, --no 3d render, photorealistic, gradient mesh, anime chibi, six fingers'.
claude_code_integration: Build a single React component `<Mascot mood={...} size={...} />` in `apps/mobile/src/components/Mascot.tsx`. It maps mood enum to Lottie/PNG asset path: `const MOOD_MAP = { idle: require('../../assets/mascot/idle.json'), celebrate: require('...'), ... }`. Use `lottie-react-native` (already a common dep). For Rive variants, install `rive-react-native` and use `<Rive resourceName='mascot' stateMachineName='Default' />`. Centralize asset paths so swapping the entire mascot is one file edit. Add a Storybook entry under `apps/mobile/.storybook/stories/Mascot.stories.tsx` so you can review all moods in one screen. Claude Code prompt: 'add a new mood variant `confused` to the Mascot component using assets/mascot/confused.json'. For preloading: in Expo, `Asset.loadAsync([require('./assets/mascot/idle.json'), ...])` in your splash bootstrap. Performance: lazy-load non-idle moods, prewarm only the idle on mount. For the React Native package size, .json Lottie files are bundled at build time (counts toward APK/IPA size); keep total mascot payload under 500KB.
emotional_design_integration: **Magician** mascot: an animal/creature with one uncanny detail — heterochromia, an extra eye, glowing subtle marking. The wonder comes from 'I can't quite tell what species'. **Creator**: maker tools/aesthetic — paint splatter, pencil behind ear, sawdust. **Jester**: extreme expression range, willingness to break the fourth wall (Duo glaring at you for missing practice). **Outlaw**: leather-jacket-energy, scar, missing tooth — but still loveable. **Hero**: confident posture, cape-energy, slight up-shot camera angle. **Caregiver**: soft round body, gentle eyes, hands-on-hips warmth (not patronizing) — Mailchimp Freddie nails this. **Innocent**: large head/body ratio, baby proportions, plain clothes. **Lover**: blush, soulful eyes, often pink palette. For our user's Magician/Creator + Jester/Outlaw + Caregiver tension: design a mascot with Caregiver body proportions (round, soft) but Jester expressions (eyebrow tilt, asymmetric smile) and Magician details (one unusual feature — third eye in marking, glow in dark stripe). This is exactly the Duolingo trick: cute owl shape + manic personality + branded green = warm-but-edgy.
specific_examples: 1. **Duolingo Duo** (https://blog.duolingo.com/building-character/) — designed by Greg Hartman since 2013; cylindrical body + half-circle wings = trivial to animate at low cost; the brand bible explicitly says 'Duo cares too much about your streak'. The shift to a less-detailed 2D Duo around 2018 dropped animation production time enormously. 2. **Mailchimp Freddie** (https://mailchimp.com/about/) — chimp head only, smiley wink, hand-drawn texture; the high-five between Freddie and the user after sending a campaign is the brand's most-quoted UX moment. 3. **GitHub Octocat / Mona** (https://octodex.github.com/) — designed by Simon Oxley as a stock cat-octopus hybrid; GitHub bought a license, then commissioned Cameron McEfee to spawn 160+ variants in the Octodex (https://blog.jakelee.co.uk/what-on-earth-are-octocats/). The variant strategy is the lesson: ONE base character spawns infinite themed riffs. 4. **Reddit Snoo** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Snoo) — alien antenna head, intentionally simple silhouette so subreddits can re-skin into millions of community variants; the most-customized mascot in tech. 5. **Discord Wumpus** (https://discord.fandom.com/wiki/Wumpus) — name from 1973 game Hunt the Wumpus; 'created as a character with no friends' is the brand's self-effacing humor. 6. **Slack Slackbot** — minimal 'tilted hash' face, intentionally neutral so the bot doesn't have personality competing with the user's tone. 7. **Trello Taco** (Trello dog mascot) — added late but became central in onboarding emails. 8. **Headspace Andy & friends** — illustrated 2D characters with hand-drawn wobble; works because hand-drawn imperfection is anti-AI-slop by construction. 9. **GitHub Copilot** — lightning-bolt-on-head Octocat variant: archetype evolution while preserving recognizable silhouette.
anti_ai_slop_traps: **Tells specific to AI-generated mascots**: (a) Generic round chibi blob — perfectly symmetrical sphere with two huge dot eyes and no character; the Midjourney/Imagen default 'cute' output. (b) Pixar-3D-blender look — glossy plastic skin, baked subsurface scattering; if it looks like a Pinterest-trending 3D render, it's slop. (c) Six-fingered hands or fused hands — still happens in 2026 if you don't gen with negative prompts. (d) Melted face features — eyes drift in size and position across generations; use IP-Adapter or LoRA to lock features. (e) Plastic doll skin with too-perfect highlight — the AI 'dewy gloss' across foreheads and noses. (f) Stuck in 3/4 view forever — AI prefers 3/4; if your library only has 3/4 poses your character feels static. Force front view, profile view, back view. (g) Identical pose with different prompt — model-mode-collapse; counter with strong pose words and ControlNet poses. (h) Pastel mauve + sage green + cream palette default. **Counter-prompts**: 'no 3D render', 'no plastic skin', 'no chibi anime', 'flat vector illustration', 'hand-drawn line weight variation', 'asymmetric facial features', 'specific expression in eyes not just smile'. **Counter-process**: enforce consistency via LoRA or IP-Adapter from day 1, not 'maybe later'; never ship a raw AI raster — always vectorize in Figma with manual touch-up. Do a 'walk test' — print 12 poses, lay them on a desk, ask 'do I believe this is one character?' If even one is from a different universe, kill it.
common_pitfalls: Designing the mascot before the bible — pretty character with no personality is dead weight. Inconsistency drift across surfaces (Lottie idle is one body shape, marketing illustration is another). Over-detailing for the icon — your mascot must survive at 40px in app icon contexts. Forgetting reduced-motion variants — Lottie ambient loops feel hostile to users with vestibular disorders if always-on. Not building dark-mode variants — black-line mascot disappears on dark backgrounds. Mascot competes with content — Slackbot is intentionally low-personality so it doesn't dominate; if your mascot is in EVERY screen at full size, it becomes annoying. Cultural pitfalls — owl is wise in Western culture, ill omen in some others; check before locking species. Gender-coding accidents — eyelashes default to 'female-coded'; consider gender-neutrality unless intentional. License hell — if you used a free vector base from Open Doodles, you have license obligations and your mascot is not unique IP. AI-trained-on-someone-else's-mascot — if you fine-tune on Duolingo screenshots, you've created derivative IP and Duolingo's lawyers will notice.
accessibility_notes: Always provide alt text for the mascot in any informational use ('illustration of a friendly owl waving'). When the mascot conveys state (mood-based), supplement with a text label or status indicator — color/expression alone is not enough for users with visual impairment or expression-recognition difficulty (autism spectrum). For animated mascots, respect prefers-reduced-motion: provide a static fallback. For Rive/Lottie, ensure accessibility roles — wrap in a View with `accessible={true}` and `accessibilityLabel`. Don't auto-play long looping mascot animations on the main screen for users on Data Saver / Low Power Mode. Cognitive load: the mascot should not appear in every screen — overuse creates visual noise that hurts users with attention difficulty.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile: native runtime (lottie-react-native, rive-react-native) handles vector animation at 60fps with low CPU; integrate via JSON/Riv files bundled at build time. Web: Lottie web runs via lottie-web (~80KB gzipped); Rive runs via rive-js (~150KB but with WASM acceleration). Mobile constraint: APK/IPA size — keep mascot assets under 500KB total; lazy-load non-idle moods. Web constraint: first-paint — the mascot Lottie shouldn't block above-the-fold render; load after hero text. Mobile-only opportunity: tilt-driven parallax (use accelerometer to make mascot eyes follow tilt). Web-only opportunity: cursor-tracking eyes (small but delightful).
